narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.71, and your Year 3 decile of 3, lower than the average 5.53. Your LSAT score of 30 also sits well below the dataset average of 36.70, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.70 stands out positively above the typical 3.21. Other details like your middle family income bracket, being female, White, and planning full-time studies align closely with common profiles—females make up about 44% of applicants, Whites around 84%, and full-time students over 92%. In weighing these, the model found your Year 3 decile to be the strongest influence, nudging the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers with stronger late-year performance. This was closely followed by your LSAT score, which had the next biggest impact in the same direction, then your Year 1 decile. Your family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status played smaller but still contributing roles, all slightly tilting toward the failure prediction rather than success. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid strengths like your GPA but notable gaps in deciles and LSAT that outweighed them here. To boost your future bar exam odds and reapplication chances, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above 37, as that's a key predictor you can directly improve with focused prep. Strengthening your academic deciles through better performance in advanced coursework could also help, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups now. Building on your already strong undergraduate GPA with targeted bar prep habits might further tip the scales in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you have clear areas of strength, and with strategic changes, a stronger prediction is within reach.
